source_paper_title: Tight Running Times for Minimum ℓq-Norm Load Balancing: Beyond Exponential Dependencies on 1/ϵ

It remains a problem to obtain tight running times when \(m = \Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for \(\theta \in (1/2, 1]\).

title: Determine tight PTAS running times for q-norm load balancing with intermediate machines
background_latex:
We consider scheduling on $m$ identical parallel machines with $n$ jobs, each job $j$ having processing time $p_j>0$. A schedule is an assignment of jobs to machines. For a schedule, let the load on machine $i$ be
\[C_i=\sum_{j\to i} p_j.\]
Fix a constant $q>1$. The optimization objective is the $q$-power sum (equivalently the $\ell_q$-norm) of loads:
\[\min \sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q \qquad \text{(equivalently minimize }(\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q)^{1/q}\text{).}\]
This problem is denoted $P\|\|\sum_i C_i^q$ in three-field scheduling notation.

An algorithm is a PTAS if for every $\varepsilon>0$ it outputs a schedule of objective value at most $(1+\varepsilon)$ times optimal in time $T(n,m,\varepsilon)$ that is polynomial in $n$ for each fixed $\varepsilon$.

We are interested in the fine-grained dependence of the best possible PTAS running time on $\varepsilon$ and on $m$ when $m$ grows as a power of $1/\varepsilon$. In particular, let
\[m = \Theta\bigl((1/\varepsilon)^\theta\bigr)\]
for a fixed exponent $\theta\in(1/2,1].\]
The paper establishes (under ETH) a nearly tight running time $2^{\tilde O(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})}+n^{O(1)}$ for the regime $m=\Theta(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})$, and shows that for sufficiently large $m$ (e.g. $m=\Omega((1/\varepsilon)\log^2(1/\varepsilon))$) there are algorithms with running time polynomial in $1/\varepsilon$; it also gives a PTAS with running time $(1/\varepsilon)^{O(m)}+n^{O(1)}$ for $m=O(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})$. The remaining regime $m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)$ for $\theta\in(1/2,1]$ is left unresolved.
problem_definition_latex:
For the scheduling problem $P\|\|\sum_{i=1}^m C_i^q$ with fixed constant $q>1$, determine the tight asymptotic running time (as a function of $\varepsilon$ and $n$) of the fastest PTAS in the parameter regime
\[m = \Theta\bigl((1/\varepsilon)^\theta\bigr)\quad\text{for a fixed }\theta\in(1/2,1].\]
In particular, either:
1) prove a matching (ETH-based) lower bound on the PTAS running time in this regime (a subexponential lower bound in $1/\varepsilon$), or
2) design a PTAS whose running time improves over the known bounds in this regime,
so as to characterize the optimal dependence on $1/\varepsilon$ for these values of $m$.

location_in_paper: Page 2, Introduction (Contribution Overview); also Page 46, Conclusion (Section H).
open_problem_quote:
It remains a problem to obtain tight running times when \(m = \Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for \(\theta \in (1/2, 1]\).

Another interesting problem is to show subexponential lower bound or develop FPTAS for \(P\|\|\sum_i C_i^q\) where \(m = \Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for \(\theta \in (1/2, 1]\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question is about proving optimal PTAS running-time bounds (upper or ETH-based lower bounds) across an asymptotic regime of $m$ versus $\varepsilon$, not about a finite extremal instance. Automated search over instances is unlikely to produce decisive evidence about tight asymptotic time dependencies.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could explore algorithmic ideas (rounding schemes, dynamic programs, MILP/configuration-LP variants) and benchmark them experimentally on instances with $m\approx (1/\varepsilon)^\theta$ to suggest improved dependencies or identify bottlenecks, providing useful conjectures. However, the core deliverable is a provable asymptotic upper bound or ETH reduction, for which fitness signals from experiments are only indirect.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Chen–Tao–Verschae, 2021) establishes a surprisingly sharp subexponential PTAS landscape for \(P\|\|\sum_i C_i^q\) with fixed \(q>1\). Building on the existence of PTASs going back to Alon–Azar–Woeginger–Yadid, it introduces a new rounding/structural lemma: grouping job sizes into \((1+\sqrt{\varepsilon})\)-bands and imposing an ordering on machines allows a Taylor-expansion argument in which the linear term cancels, yielding a \(2^{\tilde O(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})}+n^{O(1)}\) PTAS (AL2) when \(m\) is polynomial in \(1/\varepsilon\). It also gives complementary regimes: a DP-based PTAS with time \((1/\varepsilon)^{O(m)}+n^{O(1)}\) for \(m=O(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})\) (AL1), and a Karmarkar–Karp/configuration-LP inspired approach giving polynomial dependence on \(1/\varepsilon\) once \(m\) is large enough (e.g., \(m=\Omega((1/\varepsilon)\log^2(1/\varepsilon))\), AL3).

On the lower-bound side, the paper proves an ETH-based barrier matching the \(2^{\tilde O(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})}\) upper bound in the critical regime \(m=\Theta(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})\). The reduction uses new number-theoretic constructions (unique adjacent sums and linked unique sums, inspired by Sidon/Salem–Spencer sets) to keep the target load \(Q\) nearly linear in the SAT size while preserving an \(\Theta(n)\) objective gap via cancellation in \(\sum_i (Q+\Delta_i)^q\). However, the reduction inherently ties \(m\) to \(\tilde\Theta(n)\), which corresponds to \(m=\tilde\Theta(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon})\) under the choice of \(\varepsilon\) used to amplify the gap; as the authors note, it does not extend to \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for \(\theta>1/2\) because the PTAS slack \(\varepsilon\cdot mQ^q\) becomes too large relative to the gap.

Consequently, the intermediate regime \(m=\Theta((1/\varepsilon)^\theta)\) for fixed \(\theta\in(1/2,1]\) remains open: neither matching ETH lower bounds nor improved PTAS running times are known from the provided material. Promising directions suggested by the surrounding literature include adapting sparsification techniques (developed for makespan PTASs) to \(\ell_q\)-objectives, and leveraging norm-structured rounding ideas from more general minimum-norm load balancing work, to either (i) design PTASs with running time \(2^{\tilde O((1/\varepsilon)^{1-\theta})}\) or similar interpolations, or (ii) craft new gap-preserving reductions whose objective separation scales with \(m\) so that ETH lower bounds remain meaningful when \(m\) grows faster than \(\sqrt{1/\varepsilon}\).

sources.title: Approximation schemes for scheduling
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces early PTAS techniques for scheduling on identical machines, including objectives such as minimizing \(\sum_i C_i^q\) for fixed \(q>1\), with very large (doubly-exponential in \(1/\varepsilon\)) dependence on \(1/\varepsilon\).
sources.connection: Baseline PTAS existence for \(P\|\|\sum_i C_i^q\) that the Chen–Tao–Verschae paper improves upon; sets historical context for seeking tight \(\varepsilon\)-dependence.

sources.title: Approximation schemes for scheduling on parallel machines
sources.theorem_or_result: Journal version developing PTAS machinery for identical-machine scheduling; includes rounding/grouping and structural normalization (e.g., scaling so total size equals \(m\) and loads lie in a bounded interval) used as preprocessing in later work.
sources.connection: Provides the preprocessing lemma (well-structured instances) used in the source paper and underlies many PTAS running-time analyses in the intermediate-machine regime.

sources.title: On the optimality of approximation schemes for the classical scheduling problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Shows ETH-based lower bounds for PTAS running times for \(P\|\|C_{\max}\), essentially ruling out \(2^{o(1/\varepsilon)}\) dependence; develops fine-grained reduction techniques for scheduling PTAS lower bounds.
sources.connection: Key comparator: makespan has exponential-in-\(1/\varepsilon\) lower bounds, while \(\ell_q\)-norm admits subexponential PTAS; techniques and limitations inform attempts to extend lower bounds to other \(m\) regimes.

sources.title: Closing the gap for makespan scheduling via sparsification techniques
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops sparsification and rounding frameworks yielding near-optimal PTAS/EPTAS running times for makespan on identical machines, with improved dependence on \(1/\varepsilon\) and structural reductions to smaller instances.
sources.connection: Methodological backdrop: sparsification/rounding ideas that might be adaptable to \(\sum_i C_i^q\) to handle intermediate \(m\) regimes; also cited in the source paper as part of the recent running-time improvements landscape.

sources.title: Bounding the running time of algorithms for scheduling and packing problems
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies running-time bounds for approximation schemes in scheduling/packing, giving general techniques to bound state spaces and dependencies on \(1/\varepsilon\) and other parameters.
sources.connection: Provides general fine-grained PTAS running-time analysis tools relevant when trying to interpolate between regimes of \(m\) and \(\varepsilon\).

sources.title: An efficient approximation scheme for the one-dimensional bin-packing problem
sources.theorem_or_result: Classic PTAS for bin packing using configuration LP and rounding; yields additive-error control and iterative rounding ideas.
sources.connection: Underlies the source paper’s algorithmic regime for large \(m\) (their AL3 adapts Karmarkar–Karp style methods) and suggests avenues for polynomial-in-\(1/\varepsilon\) time when \(m\) is sufficiently large.

sources.title: Minimum-norm load balancing is (almost) as easy as minimizing makespan
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives approximation algorithms for minimum-norm load balancing under general norms, relating them to makespan minimization up to small losses; develops norm-aware rounding/structural arguments.
sources.connection: Although not a direct PTAS running-time result for \(\sum_i C_i^q\), it provides structural and algorithmic tools for norm objectives that may help bridge intermediate \(m\) regimes.
